title: Most Likely Suspect: The Missing Kickball

root.narration: The brand-new kickball is missing from the equipment shed. Three suspects: (1) Wyatt plays kickball every recess and was the last one to use the shed -- HIGH probability he interacted with the ball. (2) Olivia was near the shed but she only plays jump rope and has never used a kickball -- LOW probability. (3) Zach was on the other side of the playground all recess and multiple friends confirm this -- VERY LOW probability. You also know the shed was locked after recess, so it disappeared during recess when the shed was open. Who is the most probable suspect?

choices.text: Wyatt is the most probable. He plays kickball daily, was the last at the shed, and had both access and reason to have the ball. Start your investigation with him.
Investigate Olivia because she was near the shed and that is suspicious.
Investigate Zach because maybe his friends are covering for him.

outcome.headline: Probability Thinking Pays Off!
outcome.explanation: Wyatt had the highest probability: he plays kickball every day (motive to keep the ball), was the last person at the shed (opportunity), and uses kickballs regularly (means). When you asked him, he said he took the ball to practice kicks on the field and accidentally left it behind the bleachers! It was not stolen at all. By starting with the most probable suspect, you solved the case quickly instead of chasing unlikely leads.
outcome.whatToRemember: Think about probability: who is MOST LIKELY to have done it based on their habits, access, and connection to the item? Start with the highest-probability suspect.

keyTerms.definition: How likely something is to be true. A suspect with strong connections to the mystery has HIGH probability.
The person who has the best combination of motive, means, and opportunity -- making them the most probable answer.

outcome.headline: Proximity Is Not Probability!
outcome.explanation: Olivia was near the shed, but she only plays jump rope and has no connection to kickballs. Being nearby raises her probability a little, but Wyatt -- who plays kickball daily and was the last one in the shed -- has much higher probability. In detective work, you should investigate the most likely suspect first to use your time wisely.
outcome.whatToRemember: Being near the scene is one factor, but it is not enough by itself. Consider all factors -- habits, motive, and access -- to judge probability.

outcome.headline: Very Low Probability! Wrong Lead!
outcome.explanation: Multiple friends confirmed Zach was on the other side of the playground. His probability is very low -- he had no access and no connection to the kickball. Suspecting his friends are all lying is possible but extremely unlikely. Meanwhile, Wyatt had the ball in his hands during recess! Always start with the highest-probability suspect, not the lowest.
outcome.whatToRemember: Do not chase low-probability suspects when there is a high-probability one right in front of you. Follow the evidence, not suspicion.
